Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Agent at rows 30-35, blocks at Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 25-28. Earlier UP moved blocks but now it doesn't - need to investigate. The agent may need to be aligned with blocks to grab them.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: First try moving agent UP to see if it works now, then try to grab and reposition blocks.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 74 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, UP worked this time! The agent moved from rows 30-35 to 24-29. The blocks stayed at the same positions:
- Red: rows 13-16
- Blue: rows 19-22
- Green: rows 25-28
So the blocks didn't move with the agent this time. Earlier, when I moved UP, the blocks DID move. What's different?
Earlier:
- Agent at 30-35, Red at 19-22, Blue at 25-28, Green at 31-34 (cols 42-45)
- After UP: Agent at 24-29, Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 25-28 (cols 42-45)
Now:
- Agent at 30-35, Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 25-28
- After UP: Agent at 24-29, Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 25-28
The difference is that earlier, Green was at cols 42-45 (grabbed/extended), while now Green is at cols 18-21 (not grabbed). When Green was grabbed, moving the agent caused the ungrabbed blocks to move. When Green is not grabbed, moving the agent doesn't cause any blocks to move.
So the rule is: when the agent is holding a block (path extended to it), moving the agent causes the OTHER blocks to move with the agent. The held block stays in place.
This is the key mechanic! To rearrange blocks:
1. Grab a block (extend RIGHT)
2. Move the agent - this moves the other blocks
3. Release the block (retract LEFT) - this places the held block at the agent's position
Let me try this:
1. Grab Green (extend RIGHT when aligned with Green)
2. Move agent UP - this moves Red and Blue up, but Green stays
3. Retract LEFT - this places Green at the agent's position
Current state:
- Agent: rows 24-29
- Red: rows 13-16
- Blue: rows 19-22
- Green: rows 25-28
To grab Green, I need to align with it. The agent is at rows 24-29, and Green is at rows 25-28. They overlap at rows 25-28. So I should be able to grab Green by extending RIGHT.
Let me try:
1. Extend RIGHT to grab Green
2. Move UP - Red and Blue should move up, Green stays
3. Retract LEFT - Green should be placed at agent's position
After step 2:
- Agent: rows 18-23
- Red: rows 7-10 (moved up 6)
- Blue: rows 13-16 (moved up 6)
- Green: rows 25-28 (stays)
After step 3:
- Agent: rows 18-23
- Red: rows 7-10
- Blue: rows 13-16
- Green: rows 18-23 (placed at agent's position)
Wait, but Green would be at the same position as Blue (rows 13-16 vs 18-23). That's not right.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The target order is Red, Green, Blue (top to bottom). Currently it's Red, Blue, Green.
If I grab Green and move the agent UP, Red and Blue move up but Green stays. Then when I retract, Green is placed at the agent's position.
But I want Green to be between Red and Blue. So I need:
- Red at some position (top)
- Green at some position (middle)
- Blue at some position (bottom)
Currently:
- Red: 13-16
- Blue: 19-22
- Green: 25-28
If I grab Green and move UP once:
- Agent: 18-23
- Red: 7-10
- Blue: 13-16
- Green: 25-28 (stays)
Then retract:
- Green placed at agent's position (18-23)
Result:
- Red: 7-10
- Blue: 13-16
- Green: 18-23
Order: Red, Blue, Green (still wrong!)
Hmm, the relative order didn't change. The issue is that all blocks move together when the agent moves, so their relative order stays the same.
Wait, but the held block stays in place. So if I grab Green and move UP, Green stays at 25-28 while Red and Blue move up. Then when I retract, Green is placed at the agent's position (18-23).
Result:
- Red: 7-10
- Blue: 13-16
- Green: 18-23
Order: Red, Blue, Green (still wrong!)
The issue is that Red and Blue both moved up, maintaining their relative order. Green was placed below them.
To swap Blue and Green, I need to:
1. Grab Blue
2. Move agent DOWN - this moves Red and Green down, but Blue stays
3. Retract LEFT - Blue is placed at agent's position
Let me trace:
Current:
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 13-16
- Blue: 19-22
- Green: 25-28
Step 1: Grab Blue (need to align with Blue first)
- Move UP to align with Blue (19-22): Agent at 18-23
- Extend RIGHT to grab Blue
Step 2: Move DOWN - Red and Green move down, Blue stays
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 19-22 (moved down 6)
- Green: 31-34 (moved down 6)
- Blue: 19-22 (stays)
Wait, Red and Blue would both be at 19-22. That's a collision. Let me reconsider.
Actually, I think the blocks can't overlap. So if Red moves to 19-22 and Blue is already there, something else happens.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to swap Blue and Green, let me try to move Green to the top position (above Red).
Current:
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 13-16
- Blue: 19-22
- Green: 25-28
Step 1: Grab Green (extend RIGHT)
Step 2: Move UP twice - Red and Blue move up, Green stays
- Agent: 12-17
- Red: 1-4 (moved up 12)
- Blue: 7-10 (moved up 12)
- Green: 25-28 (stays)
Step 3: Retract LEFT - Green placed at agent's position (12-17)
Result:
- Red: 1-4
- Blue: 7-10
- Green: 12-17
Order: Red, Blue, Green (still wrong!)
The relative order of Red and Blue is maintained, and Green is placed below them. I can't change the relative order of Red and Blue this way.
Wait, maybe I need to grab a different block. Let me try grabbing Red instead.
Current:
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 13-16
- Blue: 19-22
- Green: 25-28
Step 1: Move UP twice to align with Red (13-16): Agent at 12-17
Step 2: Grab Red (extend RIGHT)
Step 3: Move DOWN twice - Blue and Green move down, Red stays
- Agent: 24-29
- Blue: 31-34 (moved down 12)
- Green: 37-40 (moved down 12)
- Red: 13-16 (stays)
Step 4: Retract LEFT - Red placed at agent's position (24-29)
Result:
- Red: 24-29
- Blue: 31-34
- Green: 37-40
Order: Red, Blue, Green (still wrong!)
Hmm, the relative order of Blue and Green is maintained, and Red is placed above them.
I think the issue is that I can only move the held block to the agent's position, but the other blocks maintain their relative order. So I can't swap two blocks that are both unheld.
Wait, let me reconsider. When I grab a block and move the agent, the unheld blocks move with the agent. The held block stays. When I retract, the held block is placed at the agent's position.
So if I grab the middle block (Blue) and move the agent, the top block (Red) and bottom block (Green) move with the agent, but Blue stays. Then when I retract, Blue is placed at the agent's position.
Current:
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 13-16
- Blue: 19-22
- Green: 25-28
Step 1: Move UP to align with Blue (19-22): Agent at 18-23
Step 2: Grab Blue (extend RIGHT)
Step 3: Move DOWN - Red and Green move down, Blue stays
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 19-22 (moved down 6)
- Green: 31-34 (moved down 6)
- Blue: 19-22 (stays)
But Red and Blue would both be at 19-22. This is a collision. Maybe the game prevents this by not allowing the move, or by pushing one of them.
Actually, wait. When I move DOWN, the unheld blocks (Red and Green) move down. Blue is held, so it stays. But Red moves from 13-16 to 19-22, which is where Blue is. So there's a collision.
Maybe the game handles this by stacking the blocks or by not allowing the move. Let me try it and see what happens.
Actually, I realize I need to first align the agent with Blue before grabbing it. The agent is at 24-29, and Blue is at 19-22. They don't overlap. So I need to move UP to align.
Let me try:
1. Move UP to 18-23 (overlaps with Blue at 19-22)
2. Extend RIGHT to grab Blue
3. Move DOWN - Red and Green move down, Blue stays
4. Retract LEFT - Blue placed at agent's position
But there's still the collision issue. Let me try a different approach.
Maybe I need to move the agent further DOWN before retracting. This would give more space between the blocks.
Actually, let me just try the sequence and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: UP worked - agent moved from 30-35 to 24-29. Blocks stayed in place. Key insight: blocks only move when agent is holding another block (path extended). When holding a block, moving the agent shifts unheld blocks but held block stays.
Current: Agent 24-29, Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 25-28. Target: Red, Green, Blue.
Plan: Grab Blue (middle block), move agent to reposition, then retract to place Blue at new position. This should swap Blue and Green.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: Agent 24-29, Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 25-28
# Need to swap Blue and Green to get Red, Green, Blue order
# Strategy: Grab Blue, move agent DOWN past Green, retract to place Blue below Green

# Step 1: Move UP to align with Blue (19-22)
predict(expect_change=True, note='Align agent with Blue')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After UP ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Step 2: Extend RIGHT to grab Blue
predict(expect_change=True, note='Grab Blue')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\n=== After RIGHT (grab Blue) ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Step 3: Move DOWN - Red and Green should move down, Blue stays
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move DOWN - unheld blocks move, Blue stays')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\n=== After DOWN ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Step 4: Retract LEFT to place Blue at agent's position
predict(expect_change=True, note='Retract to place Blue')
action(['LEFT'] * 5)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\n=== After retract ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:

  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 18-23

=== After RIGHT (grab Blue) ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21

=== After DOWN ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 24-29

=== After retract ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 18-21

Plan: Grab Blue (middle block), move agent to reposition, then retract to place Blue at new position. This should swap Blue and Green.
